sn22.94:1.1: Near Sāvatthī. There the Blessed One said,
sn22.94:1.2: “Monks, it’s not that I dispute with the world, but that the world disputes with me.
sn22.94:1.3: A proponent of the Dhamma doesn’t dispute with anyone with regard to the world.
sn22.94:1.4: Whatever is agreed upon by the wise as not existing in the world, of that I too say, ‘It doesn’t exist.’
sn22.94:1.5: Whatever is agreed upon by the wise as existing in the world, of that I too say, ‘It exists.’
sn22.94:2.1: “And what is agreed upon by the wise as not existing in the world that I too say, ‘It doesn’t exist’?
sn22.94:2.2: “Form that’s constant, permanent, eternal, not subject to change is agreed upon by the wise as not existing in the world, and I too say, ‘It doesn’t exist.’
sn22.94:2.3: “Feeling that’s constant, permanent, eternal, not subject to change is agreed upon by the wise as not existing in the world, and I too say, ‘It doesn’t exist.’
sn22.94:2.4: “Perception that’s constant, permanent, eternal, not subject to change is agreed upon by the wise as not existing in the world, and I too say, ‘It doesn’t exist.’
sn22.94:2.5: “Fabrications that are constant, permanent, eternal, not subject to change are agreed upon by the wise as not existing in the world, and I too say, ‘They don’t exist.’
sn22.94:2.7: “Consciousness that’s constant, permanent, eternal, not subject to change is agreed upon by the wise as not existing in the world, and I too say, ‘It doesn’t exist.’
sn22.94:3.1: “And what is agreed upon by the wise as existing in the world that I too say, ‘It exists’?
sn22.94:3.2: “Form that’s inconstant, stressful, subject to change is agreed upon by the wise as existing in the world, and I too say, ‘It exists.’
sn22.94:3.3: “Feeling that’s inconstant, stressful, subject to change is agreed upon by the wise as existing in the world, and I too say, ‘It exists.’ “Perception that’s inconstant, stressful, subject to change is agreed upon by the wise as existing in the world, and I too say, ‘It exists.’ “Fabrications that are inconstant, stressful, subject to change are agreed upon by the wise as existing in the world, and I too say, ‘They exist.’
sn22.94:3.4: “Consciousness that’s inconstant, stressful, subject to change is agreed upon by the wise as existing in the world, and I too say, ‘It exists.’
sn22.94:4.1: “Monks, there is a world-phenomenon in the world that the Tathāgata directly awakens to, breaks through to.
sn22.94:4.2: Directly awakening to & breaking through to that, he declares it, teaches it, describes it, sets it forth. He reveals it, explains it, makes it plain.
sn22.94:5.1: And what is a world-phenomenon in the world that the Tathāgata directly awakens to, breaks through to, that—directly awakening to & breaking through to it—he declares, teaches, describes, sets forth, reveals, explains, makes plain?3
sn22.94:5.2: “Form is a world-phenomenon in the world that the Tathāgata directly awakens to, breaks through to.
sn22.94:5.3: Directly awakening to & breaking through to that, he declares it, teaches it, describes it, sets it forth. He reveals it, explains it, makes it plain.
sn22.94:6.1: Whoever—when that is being declared, taught, described, set forth, revealed, explained, & made plain by the Tathāgata—doesn’t know, doesn’t see, then what can I do for that fool, that run-of-the-mill person: blind, without eye-sight, not knowing, not seeing?
sn22.94:6.2: “Feeling is a world-phenomenon in the world.…
sn22.94:6.3: “Perception is a world-phenomenon in the world.…
sn22.94:6.5: “Consciousness is a world-phenomenon in the world that the Tathāgata directly awakens to, breaks through to.
sn22.94:6.6: Directly awakening to & breaking through to that, he declares it, teaches it, describes it, sets it forth. He reveals it, explains it, makes it plain.
sn22.94:7.1: Whoever—when that is being declared, taught, described, set forth, revealed, explained, & made plain by the Tathāgata—doesn’t know, doesn’t see, then what can I do for that fool, that run-of-the-mill person: blind, without eye-sight, not knowing, not seeing?
sn22.94:8.1: “Monks, just as a blue, red, or white lotus—born in the water, grown up in the water—stands having risen above the water, unsmeared by the water;
sn22.94:8.2: in the same way, the Tathāgata—born in the world, grown up in the world—dwells having conquered the world, unsmeared by the world.”
